focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

By eight in the morning the front step had turned to black glass again.

Noreen saw it through the lace where the frost had not sealed the pane yet: a hard strip of shine from threshold to curb, no grit on it, no ashes, nothing the council promised in handbills and never managed on Barrow Lane unless a man from the council lived on that street. Peter had left heel marks from when he came in at half one, weaving and broad-backed in the hall, one shoe missing and his breath full of stout and peppermint. Those marks had frozen into pale commas in the dark.

She set his mug on the table anyway. Tea gone stewed and bitter because she had brewed it early and forgotten it while she pulled her stockings over swollen ankles that did not look like her ankles anymore. She had her clinic card tucked in the prayer book on the shelf, not for piety, for safety. Peter never opened it unless visitors came and he wanted the room to look decent.

The kettle rattled. In the spare room Arthur coughed once, turned a page, and coughed again. He had taken to reading railway timetables in the morning, stubbornly tracking hours trains no longer kept. Noreen loved him for that useless hope and pitied him for it.

Peter came down with the careful dignity of a man pretending he did not have a hangover. He had shaved a path through one cheek and left white stubble on the other. His glasses sat crooked. He put both palms on the table and looked at the tea like she had served him an insult.

"You let that stand," he said.

"I’ll pour fresh. Sit down before you tip over."

"I am not infirm." He drew himself up, then winced at the kitchen light. "Do not begin your fussing."

There it was, the schoolmaster voice, his collar-button voice. If he had gone soft in the mouth with drink he would...
prev_tail_excerpt:
..."

"If either man asks, he gets pipes and weather and nothing else," Elsie said. "I'm not handing Tom another club, and you don't hand Peter one."

Noreen gave a short laugh with no warmth in it. "Peter would write it in his little ledger and call it proof I'm unfit. Tom would drink it and spit it back at you. So they get frost and plumbing."

Elsie looked down into the tea and saw the wavering reflection of her own face beside Noreen's. "If one of us says stop, we stop. Right then. No debt after. No sulk."

Noreen's face softened. "All right. Stop means stop, and nobody pays for saying it."

Elsie tightened her grip on Noreen's hand. "If your house turns bad, you come through my door. If mine does, I come through yours. Door first."

Noreen set the mug down and took Elsie's hand. "Door first, questions after the kettle's on."

They stood there hand in hand until the cold bit through the floorboards into their feet.

Noreen glanced at the grate, then at the coal scuttle. "If we keep this fed till light, we'll have nothing left for breakfast."

Elsie nodded.

"We bank it now and take the back room. Bed keeps heat better than this floor."

Elsie hesitated just long enough to make the choice again. "All right."

They pinched the lamp wick low, banked the coals to a dull red, and carried blankets upstairs one armful at a time. The back room smelled of old lavender and damp plaster. The iron bedframe complained when they climbed in, but it held warmth better than the mattress by the grate.

Noreen turned onto her side toward the window and caught Elsie's wrist under the blanket. "Before men, remember."

"Before men," Elsie said.

The house settled around them. Somewhere down the landing a floorboard gave one long complaint, and both of them went still until the noise passed.
